title: Fitting a window board with scribed return ends
subtitle: Fix a timber window sill board with scribed ends that follow the wall plaster
excerpt: How to fit a timber window board to an internal window sill, scribing the board ends to follow the plaster reveals, cutting a rounded front nosing, and securing the board with screws and adhesive for a clean, gap-free finish.
sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: The internal timber sill of a window, fitted across the bottom of the window opening between the two side reveals. Also called a window sill board or window stool in older joinery terminology. It provides a flat surface across the masonry sill and is typically painted to match the window frame.

The shaped end of a window board that is cut to follow the irregular profile of the plaster reveal, eliminating the gap between the board end and the wall. A compass scribes the wall profile onto the board end, and a coping saw cuts to the scribed line.

content.text: sits on the masonry sill and fills the gap between the bottom of the window frame and the wall below. The board ends are

to follow the plaster reveals at each side, which are rarely at exactly 90 degrees. The front edge of the board projects 25 to 30 mm in front of the plaster face below and is typically rounded or bullnosed.

Timber for the board: standard window-board stock is 25 mm thick PAR softwood in widths from 150 to 300 mm, depending on the sill depth. MDF window-board sections are also available pre-primed. The board is cut overlong and trimmed to the scribed length. Decorator's caulk for the joint between the board ends and the plaster reveals. Construction adhesive for fixing the board to the masonry sill. No. 8 x 50 mm screws for additional fixing through the frame.
Step 1, measure the sill and cut the board to rough length

Measure the distance between the plaster faces at each reveal. Add 40 mm to this measurement for the scribing allowance: 20 mm of extra length at each end. Cut the board to this rough length. Check the board for level across the masonry sill using the spirit level. Masonry sills are often tilted slightly toward the room (a positive drainage angle); pack the board level with thin timber wedges where needed.
Step 2, scribe the board ends to the reveals

Hold the board in position, resting on the sill, butted against the back of the window frame, with equal overhang at both ends. Use a compass set to the maximum gap between the board end and the plaster reveal. Run the compass along the reveal, scribing the plaster profile onto the board end. Repeat at the other end. Cut to the scribed lines with the coping saw: cut just outside the line and pare back to it with the chisel and block plane. The fit must be tight enough that the gap is invisible or closed by the caulk bead.

The front edge of the board projects 25 to 30 mm in front of the plaster face below. This projection is typically rounded with a bullnose profile. Work the curve with the block plane: take diagonal strokes across the corner of the timber to remove the corner, then refine with the sanding block through 80, 120, and 180 grit. A consistent radius across the full width gives a professional result.

Apply construction adhesive to the masonry sill in a zigzag pattern. Press the board into position and check it is level and at the correct projection. Drive two No. 8 x 50 mm screws from below the window frame into the top back face of the board, if the frame allows it. Hold the board with masking tape at the ends while the adhesive grips. Once set, run a bead of decorator's caulk along the joint between the board ends and the plaster reveals and tool it flush.

For a bay window, the board is made in three sections with mitred corners at the angles of the bay. The mitres must be cut accurately to the angle of the bay; a sliding bevel set to the bay angle helps mark the cut. In new build, window boards are often fixed by the window installer before the plaster goes on; the scribing step is not needed where the reveals are plumb and the board was installed first.

Repaint the window board when the decoration around the window is refreshed. Check the caulk joint at the ends annually: the board moves slightly with central-heating cycles and the caulk can crack. A fresh bead of decorator's caulk over the old is sufficient if the gap is small.

attrs.symptom: Gap persists at the board end after scribing and fitting
attrs.cause: Scribe compass was set to the wrong maximum gap; or the board moved between scribing and final fitting.
attrs.fix: Fill the gap with a flexible decorator's caulk. If the gap is wider than 5 mm, re-scribe and re-cut the board end.

attrs.symptom: Board lifts at the front edge after fitting
attrs.cause: Adhesive has not bonded to the masonry sill due to dust or a powdery sill surface.
attrs.fix: Lift the board, prime the masonry sill with PVA diluted 1:4, allow to dry, and re-apply the adhesive. Add a screw fixing at the front edge if the sill allows it.

attrs.symptom: Board is not level side to side after fitting
attrs.cause: Masonry sill is not level; no packing was used.
attrs.fix: Lift and re-fix with timber packers under the low end. The packers remain under the board permanently; cut them from thin offcuts and glue them to the sill before fitting the board.
